ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried rice food product class (FOODON_00002694); no specific assorted/mixed fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fried rice as a category. No specific assorted fried rice with sausage subcode exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for assorted fried rice. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular prepared-dish codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of assorted fried rice weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-person serving as served in Singaporean Chinese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Chinese Style — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Chinese-style fried rice with egg and mixed ingredients. The assorted fried rice variant with Chinese sausage is closely analogous; values are adapted from HPB fried rice entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice and Chinese sausage components. Per-100g values represent a composite estimate for a typical assorted fried rice preparation as served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — White rice, fried, Chinese restaurant style
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for assorted fried rice with Chinese sausage. GI of 64 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Chinese-style fried rice (white rice base, stir-fried with egg and soy sauce), which is the closest analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 88.9) / 100 ≈ 56.9, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.4 × 3.5 = 88.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and Chinese sausage
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (1,470 mg per standard 350 g serving), assorted fried rice contributes a substantial proportion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced soy sauce are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 64 (medium) and a glycemic load of approximately 56.9 per standard serving, assorted fried rice can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, consider substituting brown rice, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from Chinese sausage and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g) from Chinese sausage and high sodium content raises cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving frequency and choose preparations with leaner protein substitutes and reduced-sodium soy sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Assorted Fried Rice (什锦炒饭, Shí Jǐn Chǎo Fàn) is a popular Chinese and Singaporean Chinese dish consisting of pre-cooked white rice stir-fried at high heat in a wok with a variety of ingredients including Chinese sausage (lap cheong), egg, mixed vegetables, garlic, spring onion, and soy sauce. The wok hei technique — imparting a characteristic smoky, slightly charred aroma from high-flame cooking — is central to the dish's flavour profile. The texture is loose and slightly chewy, with individual rice grains remaining separate rather than clumping. The taste is savoury and umami-rich, with subtle sweetness from the Chinese sausage and a light smokiness from the wok. In Singapore, assorted fried rice is a staple comfort food served in Chinese zi char restaurants, hawker centres, and casual dining establishments. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 602 kcal, 18.2 g protein, 20.3 g fat, and 88.9 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (approximately 1,470 mg per serving) due to soy sauce and cured sausage, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its medium glycemic index (GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving make it a food requiring careful portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to egg and pork sausage content, and is not halal due to the use of Chinese pork sausage.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried rice?
Is fried rice high in sodium?
What is the GI of fried rice?
What ingredients are in Chinese fried rice?
How is wok fried rice made?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for assorted fried rice with Chinese sausage was available; values are adapted from HPB Singapore fried rice entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Chinese-style fried rice. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from cross-referenced USDA SR Legacy data where defensible. The dish contains Chinese pork sausage (lap cheong) and is therefore not halal and not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Sodium content is notably high due to soy sauce and cured sausage; this is a primary clinical concern. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on Chinese-style fried rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for assorted fried rice with Chinese sausage was located in the approved GI source. Glycemic load per serving = (64 × 88.9) / 100 ≈ 56.9 g, based on a 350 g serving containing 88.9 g carbohydrate.
